title: Why Rules-Based Orders Fail
gist: Benn Steil argues, via an analogy to Hofstadter's Godel-incompleteness theme in 'Godel, Escher, Bach,' that any rules-based order complex enough to govern itself will eventually face questions its own rules cannot resolve, so the post-Cold-War assumption of a self-sustaining liberal order was naive.

insights.claim: The post-Cold-War American belief that the liberal international order had become self-sustaining was a temporary illusion rather than a durable structural fact.
insights.speaker: Benn Steil
insights.so_what: Treat the durability of the rules-based order as a contested assumption when pricing geopolitical and sovereign risk, not a given backstop.
insights.quote: For a brief period after the Cold War, Americans persuaded themselves that the liberal order had become self-sustaining.
insights.locator: Standfirst, 'For a brief period after the Cold War'

insights.claim: Any rules-based system elaborate enough to govern and interpret its own operations will inevitably confront questions its own rules cannot answer, mirroring Godel's incompleteness as popularized in Hofstadter's 'Godel, Escher, Bach.'
insights.speaker: Benn Steil
insights.so_what: Use the incompleteness frame to anticipate that institutional crises arise from unresolvable internal contradictions, not just external shocks.
insights.quote: any rules-based system elaborate enough to govern and interpret its own operations will eventually confront questions that its rules cannot answer

insights.claim: Systems that appear solid and self-sustaining may contain contradictions they can never resolve from within, per Steil's reading of Hofstadter.
insights.speaker: Benn Steil
insights.so_what: When analyzing institutional or market frameworks, look for built-in contradictions as the source of eventual breakdown.
insights.quote: Though systems may appear solid and self-sustaining, they also may contain contradictions that they can never resolve from within.
